images.caption:
Figure 1:Illustration of token-level sign-consistency gating on a
correct math rollout. Since the final answer is verified as correct,
the outcome-level GRPO advantagea1a_{1}is positive for the trajectory.
However, the OPD reverse-KL advantagea2a_{2}can still vary by token:
consensus tokens such as “75%”, “÷\div”, and “16” havea1​a2>0a_{1}a_{2}>0and are extrapolated, while a redundant verification token
such as “Check:” can receivea2<0a_{2}<0because the teacher assigns it
lower probability, so SG-OPD routes it through interpolation.

Figure 2:Sample-level Phased Teacher Sampling (PTS). A mini-batch is
split into student on-policy rollouts and a small fraction of teacher
rollouts. Verified teacher trajectories are retained
and used as an auxiliary CE anchor, while incorrect teacher
trajectories are discarded. The teacher-guidance weight is annealed
from warm-up to zero, so the asymptotic training distribution remains
on-policy.

Figure 3:Overview of token-level sign-consistency gating. GRPO and OPD token advantages are routed by sign agreement:
consensus tokens are extrapolated, while conflict tokens are softened by interpolation.

Figure 4:Per-benchmark avg@3232accuracy (%) under the
strong-to-weak setting (Qwen3-1.7B distilled from
Qwen3-4B-Non-Thinking-RL-Math). The light-to-dark blue gradient
ranges over the off-/on-policy distillation baselines (SFT, OPD,
ExOPD);SG-OPD(red) consistently leads on AIME and on
average.

Figure 5:Training dynamics under the same setup as
Tab.1for the three on-policy distillation regimes:OPD(no extrapolation,λ=1.0\lambda{=}1.0),ExOPD(uniform extrapolation,λ=1.25\lambda{=}1.25), andSG-OPD(token-level sign-consistency gating withλhigh=1.8\lambda_{\mathrm{high}}{=}1.8).(a)Training reward: SG-OPD reaches and maintains the
highest plateau, while OPD converges to the lowest.(b)Mean response length: OPD generates noticeably shorter
trajectories than the two extrapolation-based variants.(c)Policy entropy: SG-OPD preserves substantially higher
entropy throughout training, consistent with its sign-consistency-gated
extrapolation amplifying consensus tokens without collapsing
exploration.

header: Appendix BVariance Analysis of the Token-Level Approximation

images.caption:
Figure 6:Per-benchmark avg@3232accuracy under the strong-to-weak
setting (Qwen3-1.7B distilled from Qwen3-4B-Non-Thinking-RL-Math).
Four configurations are shown:OPD(dark gray,λ=1.0\lambda{=}1.0),ExOPDat the best uniform setting (blue,λ=1.25\lambda{=}1.25),ExOPDat an aggressive uniform strength
(orange,λ=1.8\lambda{=}1.8, “untrainable” regime), and ourSG-OPD(red,λhigh=1.8\lambda_{\mathrm{high}}{=}1.8). Uniform
aggressive extrapolation collapses across all four benchmarks,
while SG-OPD recovers the same extrapolation strength via
sign-consistency gating and improves over both OPD and ExOPD.

Figure 7:Sign-agreement diagnostic logged inside the gate
for the SG-OPD run. Of the tokens with
non-zero advantages,3030+34≈47%\frac{30}{30+34}\!\approx\!47\%are amplified
and53%53\%are softened, in stable ratio across training. Naive
additive ExOPD+\!+\!RL would propagatebothstreams without
distinction; SG-OPD routes the34%34\%disagree mass throughinterpwithβ=1\beta{=}1and the30%30\%agree mass through
extrapolationλhigh=1.8\lambda_{\mathrm{high}}{=}1.8. The36%36\%remaining
mass corresponds to tokens with either advantage≈0{\approx}0and
is unaffected by the gate. The disagree share never falls below31%31\%, consistent with keeping the gate active throughout training
rather than only at warm-up.
